name: Oyá
description: Oyá, also known as Yansan or Yansã, is a powerful orisha (deity) in Yoruba religion and its diasporic traditions like Santería and Candomblé. She is the orisha of winds, storms, lightning, and violent rain, often associated with the Niger River, which is also known as the River Oyá. Oyá is a fierce warrior and a protector of women. She is believed to be a shapeshifter, capable of transforming into animals, particularly the buffalo. Oyá is also associated with death, rebirth, and transformation, acting as a psychopomp who guides souls to the afterlife alongside her husband, Ògún. She is a dynamic force of nature, representing change, progress, and the power of the feminine spirit. Oyá's temperament is often described as fiery and independent, reflecting the untamed nature of the elements she commands. She is a force to be reckoned with, capable of both destruction and profound renewal. Offerings to Oyá often include eggplant, red wine, and copper items.
subtitle: Goddess of Winds, Storms, and Transformation

keyMyths.title: Oyá and Ògún's Partnership
keyMyths.description: Oyá and Ògún worked together to usher spirits into the realm of the dead. Ògún clears the path while Oyá guides the spirits safely.

keyMyths.title: Oyá's Buffalo Transformation
keyMyths.description: Oyá possesses the power to transform into a buffalo, showcasing her immense strength and connection to the animal kingdom. This transformation is often depicted in stories of her battling adversaries.

keyMyths.title: Oyá and Shango's Love
keyMyths.description: Oyá is known as the favorite wife of Shango, the orisha of thunder and lightning. Their relationship represents a powerful union of natural forces and mutual respect.

sources.author: John Mason

sources.description: A collection of songs and prayers dedicated to various orishas, including Oyá.
sources.text: A collection of songs and prayers dedicated to various orishas, including Oyá.
